data.title: The Dollar's Handbrake Turn
data.description:
By Chris Turner

The solid labour market that wasn’t

We should have known something was up when Federal Reserve dissenters Chris Waller and Michelle Bowman released rare joint statements 30 minutes ahead of the jobs data, explaining their dovish position. One could be forgiven for thinking this was an attempt to exert maximum pressure on Chair Powell should the jobs numbers prove weak – which they were. As James Knightley discusses here, the huge back-month revisions now undermine the Fed’s key stance that the labour market has been solid and that a preemptive rate cut was not needed.

The huge drop in short-dated rates (now compounded by a resoundingly soft set of ISM manufacturing data) has triggered 9 bp of bullish steepening in the US Treasury curve on the view that the Fed will be forced to act in September. As James writes, it looks hard to argue with a Fed cut then unless there are some sharp upside surprises in the inflation data or this soft jobs data somehow reverses in the August release.

Corrective dollar high looks to be in place

Wednesday’s bearish flattening in the curve, which sent the dollar 1% higher across the board, seems like a distant memory. And that’s fair because today’s data has provided a reset on Fed thinking. Leading the dollar lower today has been USD/JPY, where the soft payrolls report has knocked 1.8% off the S&P 500 so far – presumably on the view that US domestic demand might not be quite as resilient as first thought – and perhaps faces further challenges as US businesses are forced to pass tariff hikes through to consumers this quarter.

The story for coming months will be which of the two opposing forces dominates: e.g. will labour market data deteriorate enough to cement the September rate cut and a deeper easing cycle? Or will the pick-up in tariff-driven inflation force the Fed to continue favouring modestly restrictive policy? The size of today’s backward revisions in the data suggests the momentum will be with the former.

If equities stay offered, it will be the likes of USD/JPY and USD/CHF which lead the dollar lower again. However, calling a top in US equities on the back of some softish payroll data – and a Fed potentially cutting rates – remains a dangerous game given the powerful forces in play. That’s why we think there could be continued interest in emerging market currencies and dollar-funded carry trade strategies as we discuss here.

What seems a little clearer to us is that a corrective high is in place for the dollar – e.g. just below 1.14 for EUR/USD and close to 151 in USD/JPY. These weren’t too far from the levels we discussed in July’s FX talking publication. We hadn’t expected EUR/USD to push back into the 1.18/20 until much later in the year. But depending on whether the labour market data deteriorates further – enough for the Fed to increasingly look through the temporary pick-up in inflation – those higher levels could be seen much sooner.

We’ll also keep a close look on the FX option market, where a continued rise in three-month traded volatility and the risk reversals being bid up even further in favour of euro calls will tell us that the EUR/USD rally has legs. For reference, the three-month risk reversal skew for euro calls is now 0.43% versus a bullish peak in May of over 1%.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Bristol Myers: Dividend Growth, Raised Guidance, And A Double Beat
data.description:
Editor's Note: This article was published on August 01, 2025 at 8:30 AM ET. Due to a production error, it was incorrectly dated July 01, 2025. In order to correct the date, the timestamp has changed to 5:00 PM ET, but the content is unchanged.]

Bristol Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY) has been a destroyer of shareholder value as its share price has declined by -28.70% over the past decade while the S&P 500 has rallied just over 208%. The market sentiment on BMY has been poor and no matter how much profitability they generate or capital they return to shareholders has changed the market’s perception on them. BMY delivered a double beat this earnings season as revenue came in at $12.27 billion which was $890 million ahead of the consensus estimates. BMY’s non-GAAP EPS was $1.46, which was a beat of $0.36. BMY also increased their full year revenue guidance by roughly $700 million. I continue to believe that the market is wrong and BMY is undervalued. Investors are getting a lot for their capital as the dividend of $2.48 is generating a 5.39% yield and the board still has an authorization of up to $5 billion on the current share repurchase program. BMY made significant progress this quarter as they achieved several clinical and regulatory milestones. BMY is focused on its growth portfolio to offset drugs coming off patent and I believe trading at under 10 times earnings is too cheap for a company that has generated $5.05 billion in net income over the trailing twelve months (TTM).

Following up on my previous article about BMY

I am absolutely shocked that BMY has fallen by -8.94% since my last article was published on 4/25 (can be read here). When the dividend is taken into account BMY’s total return is -7.74% while the market has climbed 16.73%. It’s been more of the same as BMY continues to underperform the market. In my previous article I had discussed how BMY’s growth portfolio was thriving as it was producing significant YoY revenue increases to offset revenue lost from expiring patents. I felt that the continuous dividend growth and mid-single digit yield combined with improving financials and share repurchases would be enough to cause shares of BMY to rebound. Several months later BMY is still being left out of the rally and investors are now paying under 8 times its 2025 projected earnings. I think that the pharmaceutical sector will make a comeback and BMY is positioning itself for future growth. I am following up with a new article to discuss how BMY is positioned for the future and why I see value in its current share price.

Risks to investing in BMY

While I am bullish on BMY there are several risk factors for investors to consider and everyone should do their own due diligence. BMY’s revenue is still heavily dependent on drugs such as Eliquis, Opdivo, and Revlimid which are likely to experience declining sales as generic versions hit the market. There is also immense competition in the pharmaceutical drug space and innovative therapies could reduce BMY’s market share. BMY allocates roughly $9.5 billion annually toward their R&D for future drugs and if they don’t pass the FDA clinical trials BMY could experience setbacks that cost billions. BMY is also subject to laws and regulations that could impact how they conduct business. BMY could also face future lawsuits due to product liabilities which could result in billions in settlements. BMY is also operating in a segment that hasn’t been popular for some time and the market sentiment is extremely low despite its profitability. BMY could experience years of underperforming the market and continue its previous trend from the past decade. These are all aspects for investors to consider before allocating capital toward BMY.

BMY delivered a strong quarter and I believe their positioned well for the future.

BMY demonstrated their ability to execute on their transition from a legacy portfolio to a stable of growth products. BMY’s Q2 earnings were powered by an 18% YoY increase in revenue from its growth portfolio to $6.6 billion. BMY’s revenue came in at $12.3 billion in Q1 which was a 1% increase YoY. BMY experienced strong demand across it’s brands, which included its immuno-oncology portfolio, cell therapies, hematology treatments, cardiovascular medicines, and recent neuroscience launches. The global sales for Opdivo increase 7% to $2.56 billion which was driven by successful launches in new indications. Breyanzi increased the amount of revenue it produced by 122% as it produced $344 million which solidified its position as the top CAR T therapy in the U.S. and it benefited from reduced patient monitoring requirements. Reblozyl experienced a 33% increase in revenue to $568 million while Camzyos achieved $260 million which was an increase of 86%. BMY’s legacy portfolio declined by -14% YoY to $5.7 billion which was due to generic competition as it impacted BMY’s products such as Revlimid, Pomalyst, and Sprycel. Eliquis’s helped offset the decline as it’s global sales reached $3.68 billion which was a 6% YoY increase. This is why focusing on building out BMY’s next generation of products is critical as the market is already seeing erosion in revenue from its legacy products. BMY non-GAAP diluted EPS was $1.46 which exceeded analysts estimates by 37.74% as they were looking for $1.06. I feel as if the market is overlooking the fact that BMY generated $3.9 billion in cash from operations and had $13.9 billion in cash and cash equivalents on the balance sheet which leaves them in a strong position to build out their growth portfolio.

BMY is strategically positioning itself for sustained long-term growth by focusing on its growth portfolio. The global strategic partnership with BioNTech which was announced in June is critical as they will codevelop BNT327 which is a bispecific antibody which is expected to accelerate BNT327's development across numerous solid tumor types. BMY also has a broad clinical program in progress which includes a first-line non-small cell lung cancer, small cell lung cancer, and a triple-negative breast cancer. If the clinical program goes well it could put BMY is position to be either 1st or 2nd to market and maximize the commercial opportunity which would recognize BNT327 as a critical theory. This could accelerate BMY's overall growth trajectory and help expand their earnings potential. BMY has secured an exclusive worldwide license agreement for OncoACP3 from Philochem AG which is a clinical-stage therapeutic and diagnostic agent targeting prostate cancer. BMY announced the formation of an independent biopharmaceutical company which will be known as Immunology NewCo in partnership with Bain Capital which will optimize 5 immunology assets. Of these therapies 3 are clinical stage and 2 are phase 1 ready compounds. I believe that this highlights BMY’s growth strategy as they look to also source external innovation to maximize value creation and deliver growth for shareholders.

BMY raised its guidance for the 2025 fiscal year by roughly $700 million which reflects their ability to generate long-term growth. The revision is due to a combination of the growth portfolio expanding quicker than expected and the legacy portfolio not declining as much as they originally expected it to. BMY’s strategic investments and business development deals are providing additional investment opportunities within the growth portfolio. BMY has established a multi-year plan which would put them in the top tier of growth within the pharmaceutical sector. A core component of this strategy is disciplined capital allocation as BMY is making investing in growth a top priority. BMY is also focused on reducing it’s debt load by $10 billion by the end of Q2 2026 and they have already achieved $6.5 billion of this debt being eliminated. BMY is positioning itself for the future as they are expecting numerous Phase 3 program readouts which are expected to de-risk its drug pipeline and shape its growth trajectory in the 2nd half of 2025. I believe that BMY’s financial management and pipeline of new products will evolve into a strong growth cycle as the next several years unfold. BMY is expected to deliver progress in critical areas that could lead to billions of revenue growth and allow BMY to go on the offensive instead of trying to offset a declining portfolio of assets.

Why I see a lot of value in shares of BMY

Investors are getting a large amount of capital distributed to them from BMY’s dividend which management has made a top priority. BMY is paying an annualized dividend of $2.48 with 17 years of growth tied to it. Over the last 5-years BMY has an average dividend growth rate of 6.93%. Based on the current share price BMY’s dividend yield is 5.39% and as the risk-free rate of return declines I think BMY’s dividend yield will become much more attractive. There is currently $7.4 trillion sitting on the sidelines in money market accounts and as the Fed lowers rates many investors will likely look to recreate the income they are generating from the sidelines. BMY is a strong dividend growth name that has a larger than average yield and the earnings to support future dividend growth. Investors are getting paid a large amount to endure the current fluctuations in its share price. I think the dividend provides a large value proposition for shareholders especially as a lower rate environment will only make the dividend look more attractive.

Here is where my contrarian view comes into play. The analyst community doesn’t expect the growth portfolio to outstrip the decline from the legacy portfolio as they see EPS decreasing over the next several years. In 2025 BMY is expected to produce $6.32 of EPS and then $5.92 of EPS in 2027. Even if they are correct investors are still paying 7.76 times 2027 earnings which is very cheap. I don’t think the analyst community is taking into consideration the impact from BMY’s investments and buisness developments in addition to what a lower rate environment could mean for BMY. The interest expenses BMY has incurred during 2024 was $1.96 billion and in the TTM they have exceeded $2 billion. By this time next year BMY expects to eliminate another $4.5 billion of debt and the Fed is expected to lower rates by 100bps by the end of 2026. In a lower rate environment BMY can issue new debt and repay the existing debt that has larger yields which would lower interest expenses and expand earnings. There is a combination of things that can go right over the next several years that I don’t believe are being priced in and BMY could be an overlooked gem.

Conclusion

BMY is not on many investors radars as the chart is horrible. With the amount of gains being generated just allocating capital toward an index fund there is less reason to invest in companies like BMY. For value and income investors BMY is an opportunity as it’s being overlooked by the market. BMY is making significant progress in offsetting its declining legacy portfolio and I think the market is overlooking the opportunity in all the clinical trials that are underway. As the Fed lowers rates it could help BMY expand earnings and make their dividend more attractive to income investors. Management is focused on expanding their growth portfolio and generating value for shareholders through its dividend and share buybacks. I think that there is a lot of value to be unlocked in BMY and an opportunity to generate a strong amount of income while waiting for the narrative to change.

data.title: BUZZ: Beware The Bearish August-September Trend In Social Media Darling Stocks
data.description:
There are many macro-crosscurrents for investors to weigh right now. Tariffs are obviously front and center, with new very, high reciprocal import duties set to take effect on August 7. On the positive side of the ledger, Q2 corporate earnings growth has been fantastic, now with almost two-thirds of S&P 500 firms having reported.

The blended EPS growth rate is now above 10%, a clear sign that while the trade war is disruptive, multinational corporations continue to churn out profits and reward shareholders.

On the negative side is something more technical—seasonality. According to data going back to 1987, global stocks tend to perform quite badly in August and September. That could cast a bearish cloud over today’s hot stocks, namely high-momentum equities in the VanEck Social Sentiment ETF (NYSEARCA:BUZZ).

After a feverish pitch in recent weeks, I’m taking a cautious stance. I have a hold rating on the fund and will note its valuation and look at its technicals as we venture into a precarious period on the calendar.

Strong Q2 Earnings Season

Bearish Seasonality On Tap For Global Stocks

According to the issuer, BUZZ seeks to track the performance of the BUZZ NextGen AI US Sentiment Leaders Index, which is intended to track the performance of the 75 large-cap U.S. stocks that exhibit the highest degree of positive investor sentiment and bullish perception based on content aggregated from online sources including social media, news articles, blog posts, and other alternative datasets.

BUZZ is a small ETF with just $101 million in assets under management as of July 31, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is high at 76 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at just 0.38%. The fund garnered attention when Wall Street technician Jonathan Krinsky of BTIG profiled the product in a research note last month.

To its credit, BUZZ sports stellar share-price momentum, helping to rank it No. 1 out of 88 in its subclass by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Other metrics are not so sanguine. It’s very risky, as evidenced by elevated historical volatility readings, while liquidity can be sketchy at times. Its median 30-day bid/ask spread is 10 basis points, per VanEck.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 5-star ETF by Morningstar plots along the top of the style box and the right-side column. That means BUZZ is allocated significantly to US large-cap growth, but with some nuance by size and style. There is very little SMID-cap value, so thematic plays and momentum play key roles in the product’s performance. I’ll note later that we are seeing a slowing of absolute momentum heading into August, which is a yellow flag.

On valuation, with a price-to-earnings ratio above 28x and a long-term EPS growth rate less than 10%, the resulting PEG ratio is elevated at close to 3x.

BUZZ: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

BUZZ may not be as speculative as you might think. Among its top 10 largest positions, there are several household names, such as AMD (AMD), Alphabet (GOOGL), and NVIDIA (NVDA).

Of course, these are by no means low-beta stocks, but it’s evident that BUZZ is not a pure low-quality, high-risk portfolio. On the flip side, stocks like AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) and Robinhood (HOOD) are some of summer’s biggest movers.

BUZZ: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, BUZZ has a limited history, but from 2021 to 2024, there have been just two up months from August through September. This is clearly a nervous period for high-momentum and frothy stocks, so extra caution is warranted.

BUZZ: Weak Aug-Sept Seasonality

The Technical Take

With a lofty valuation and a bearish seasonal stretch just getting underway, BUZZ’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares are high and tight at all-time highs, near the $32 mark. What stands out to me is that there has been a volume surge in recent weeks, resulting in new traders playing the stock between $32 and $33.

So, if we see a breakdown below, say, $31, then there will be a clear resistance line in play—just take a look at the massive horizontal volume by price bar on the left side of the graph. Moreover, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart has weakened since mid-July, suggesting that the bulls are losing some control.

Of course, the long-term 200-day moving average is positively sloped, which is a bullish signal, while support may come into play between $28 and $29.

BUZZ: Weaker RSI, Key Support High $20s, Volume Spike

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on BUZZ. The broad trend is up, but a pullback to $28 could certainly be in the works as we progress through a weak calendar period.

data.title: Duke Energy: Inside The Southeast Power Demand Boom (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Duke Energy: Corporate Overview

Exploring upstream the supply chain required by the massive AI infrastructure build cycle has brought me to find several interesting stocks I have talked about on Seeking Alpha. One is the leader in advanced cables, Prysmian (OTCPK:PRYMY, OTCPK:PRYMY), which has recently seen strong momentum because it is viewed as a beneficiary of U.S. tariffs on copper. In this article, however, we will look even more upstream, focusing on what can be considered the feeding of the whole AI revolution: energy. To do so, we will look at Duke Energy Corporation (NYSE:DUK), one of the main energy companies in the United States with a significant presence in the Southeast and Midwest regions. It reports its functions under two segments: Electric Utilities and Infrastructure (EU&I) and Gas Utilities and Infrastructure (GU&I). The former carries electricity to 8.2M customers; the latter distributes natural gas to 1.6M customers, while operating pipelines and gas storage facilities. Duke's energy portfolio is well diversified, spanning from coal to nuclear power, including hydroelectric, natural gas, oil, and renewables.

Growing Power Demand Fuels Duke's Expansion Plan

When we look at the areas with the largest population gains, we see that Florida is booming, but the two Carolinas also appear. The regions Duke services are among the areas where we are seeing strong population growth, which means energy demand is going to increase above average here.

Further proof can be found in the number of building permits. Here, Florida does much better than the larger California, which is about to be overcome by North Carolina, a state with roughly a quarter of California's population.

What's fueling the shift to the Southeastern States? Well, Charlotte and Raleigh have become important technology hubs. Atlanta is a logistics center with few competitors. South Carolina is seeing a manufacturing renaissance. Florida attracts not only retirees but also offers many attractive tech and finance jobs. Housing is a bit cheaper compared to the Northeast and the West Coast, and taxes are often lower. Finally, the climate is something that shouldn't be overlooked. As a person who has lived for many years in Texas (not exactly in the Southeast, but surely a Southern state) I still remember the mild winters that, if coupled with a marine setting such as many Southeastern states offer, can be highly attractive especially as remote work now allows people to relocate wherever they perceive the best quality of life for them. Over the last few years, therefore, it has not been unusual to hear Duke saying that demand for electricity in the Carolinas is growing, driven by new manufacturing sites and a broader wave of capital projects in key sectors such as semiconductors (Wolfspeed), batteries (Toyota), biopharma (Biogen) and data centers (Apple, Google, Meta). In late June, we saw the U.S. Department of Energy declare a power emergency in the Southeast due to the strains electric grids were suffering because of a heatwave. Duke Energy informed the DoE that it might not have sufficient generation available to meet unusually high demand, making the option of curtailing electric use no longer taboo. In this environment, Duke Energy has committed to a 5-year capital plan worth $83B, targeting to build new power generation and modernize the existing transmission and distribution system. We see that the first reason to explain this huge capital plan stresses the "significant economic development from data centers and advanced manufacturing". This is no short-lived trend, and recent studies forecast that by 2028, data centers will account for up to 12% of U.S. electricity consumption. Once a data center or an advanced manufacturing facility is built, it will run for years, if not decades, before the investment is fully amortized. As a result, we can forecast that growing energy demand needs will be a constant in future years. For Duke, this means further stability and visibility. Without this clear landscape, it would make no sense to fund such a big capital plan. As an example of Duke's expansion plans, we just learned that the company is going to apply to South Carolina to build a natural gas combined cycle generating facility with hydrogen capability, the first request in over a decade.

But Duke has also given us an idea of what will happen after 2029. It believes its capex will be even higher in the second half of the next ten years, estimating at least $110B of investments. While through 2029, the grid will see 45% of the investments, from 2029 to 2034, Duke expects zero-Carbon generation to be the first target of its capex, accounting for 40% of the total.

Duke Energy's Q2 Earnings Preview

Overall, Duke enjoys a favorable economic and demographic context and is surely poised to benefit from growing power demand, although this will require Duke to invest heavily. The regulatory environment also seems stable and predictable, as Duke Energy's management often states during the earnings calls. As a result, Duke Energy confidently guided for a long-term EPS growth rate of 5% to 7% through 2029, with FY2025 EPS expected to be between $6.17 and $6.42.

The consensus expects the company's Q2 earnings to be around $1.17, even though 9 out of 10 analysts revised downwards their estimates in the last 90 days. If Duke comes close to this estimate, it is well on track to end the FY close to the high range of its EPS guidance. If we will see a severe drop, then the FY guidance will be heavily criticized. However, utilities usually deliver close to their guidance. The overall revenue consensus expects almost $7.5B in revenues, which would confirm strong pricing. However, the weather in the Southeast this year has been warmer than usual in the spring, with April and May ranking among the top 5 warmest in the Carolinas. Preliminary data for June also shows evidence that Southeastern states saw average temperatures roughly 1.5° to 2.0° °F above the average. The outlook for the summer is similar, so we could even expect a strong Q3 performance. I would thus not be surprised to see strong performance in electricity demand and revenues above $7.55B.

When we consider Duke Energy, we can't ignore the balance sheet. It carries almost $80B in LT debt, which, considering the $15B TTM EBITDA, gives us a leverage of 5.3x. Duke has usually hovered between a 5 and a 6. Although I prefer utilities ranging between a 4 and a 5, it is, in any case, an acceptable ratio. However, a risk comes from its strategy to maintain its current credit rating. As we heard during the last earnings call, Duke addressed that its FFO/debt ratio is just 100 bps above the downgrade threshold.

Our commitment to our current credit ratings and a strong balance sheet will remain a top priority as we execute our growth objectives. We are delivering on all credit supportive initiatives and are firmly on track to achieve 14% FFO to debt this year, and we expect to improve above 14% over the 5-year plan. This provides over 100 basis points of cushion above our Moody's downgrade threshold, and over 200 basis points above our S&P downgrade threshold.

This means there are some risks when investing in Duke Energy. Allow me to list a few:

  • extraordinary or higher-than-expected capex
  • regulatory and tariff delays (a postponed rate adjustment can squeeze margins for a few quarters)
  • extreme weather could unfavorably impact operating costs, since unpredictable cold snaps or heat waves can cause demand fluctuations, driving consumption either above or below what was previously anticipated, directly impacting the company's FFO.

Thus, a 100bps cushion can be tolerable, but the company has to be laser-focused on deleveraging or open secure and flexible credit facilities to cover peak funding needs. Some positive news came from a recent divestiture Duke made. The company recently agreed to sell its Piedmont Natural Gas Tennessee local distribution company to Spire (SR) for $2.48B in cash. This will help the company raise $1.5B that will help fund the massive capital plan.

Duke's dividend history is close to reaching 20 years of dividend increases. However, the last hike was a meager 2%, below the pace of inflation. It suggests that the company is going to be frugal with its shareholder returns, lest it jeopardize its balance sheet and its credit rating.

Valuation

Duke right now seems fairly priced. It trades at a fwd PE of 19, and its fwd PEG ratio is not encouraging since it reads 3. The fwd EV/EBITDA is 12, which rightly deserves a C as its factor grade. The P/FCF is lower, 7.6, but this could reflect the fact that investors are uncertain as to the company's FCF stability given its huge capex plan. When we consider its mid-single-digit growth prospects, I think these multiples are decent, but don't scream that Duke is a buy right now. The dividend could be a reason to buy the stock, but its fwd 3.5% yield is outmatched by other similar companies, and the 2% growth rate maintained in the past 5 years is surely not attractive. As a result, I think Duke right now is an interesting company to follow to keep track of the AI revolution and its consequences, but I don't see it as a convincing alpha-returning investment, although I surely don't advise selling it. Hold rating for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Jobs Revisions Shock - July's Report Also Disappoints
data.description:
NEW YORK (August 1) - The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) stunned the markets with a disappointing 73,000 new jobs for July, according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey.

The number was well below the consensus estimate of 106,000. The private payroll number was 83,000, after taking account of 10,000 government jobs lost.

Today’s report aligned with last month’s private payroll figure,

Notably, government revisions for May showed 50,000 fewer jobs and only 11,000 new jobs in June for an average of 19,500 jobs lost. After showing 73,000 new jobs in June. We note this paragraph from the June jobs report:

Employment in state government increased by 47,000, largely in education (+40,000). Employment in local government education continued to trend up (+23,000). Job losses continued in the federal government (-7,000), where employment is down by 69,000 since reaching a recent peak in January. (Employees on paid leave or receiving ongoing severance pay are counted as employed in the establishment survey.) [Emphasis is mine.]

We suspect that the Federal DOGE layoffs took effect in July after employees completed their severance period. But what shocked this analyst (and the markets) was that May and June job creation figures were revised down by 258,000 jobs. The average new non-farm job creation figure for the last three months is just 48,500 new jobs.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs, and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed well above the Establishment Survey at 200,000 new jobs.

The unemployment rate and the U6 rate both ticked up to 4.2% and 7.9%, respectively. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part time for economic reasons, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force. Some 38,000 people left the labor force, pushing the Labor Force Participation Rate down slightly, to 62.2% from 62.3% in June.

Today’s numbers made yesterday’s ADP jobs report, which showed 104,000 jobs lost, seemingly inapposite for observers of the labor economy. But June’s ADP that showed 33,000 new jobs now seems appropriate, given today’s June revisions.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of June and July Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

Jobs creation was led, once again, by what we call “government-adjacent” jobs: Private Education & Health Services and Healthcare and Social Services, which tend to be funded by governmental programs, followed by Financial Services, as accounting, banking, and other financial firms hire on new staff and recent graduates to train them for their roles. The other gainer was in lower-wage jobs in hospitality. Retail gained 15,700 jobs after losing 14,300 jobs in June, so it was a veritable "wash". Lower-wage hospitality gained 5,000 new jobs. Manufacturing lost 11,000 jobs, all in the non-durable manufacturing sector, which, of note, includes food processors that have reputedly had a large number of illegal alien laborers. Durable goods manufacturing remained at the status quo.

We do not include “official” government jobs payrolls in our chart, but the government lost 10,000 jobs, as discussed above.

Analysis

Economy Generally

Today’s numbers resuscitate our recession concerns that we had back in January. We had all but put them to rest based on the jobs reports that now seem to have been gravely misleading.

As we explained in the June jobs report:

As we explained back in January, historically, a recession is almost assured after the 3-month/10-year inverted yield curve is resolved. We’re still not yet ready to dismiss that thinking at the start of the third quarter, but we are reducing our level of confidence and judging it to be less likely than more likely.

But we added,

That said, the data of late does show the economy is slowing somewhat. First quarter GDP printed basically down at -0.5% in the third and final estimate. So-called “Real GDP” - GDP minus elements of “noise”, and formally referred to as “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers" - printed at 1.9% in the revised estimate.

That “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers” printed at just 1.2% in the preliminary estimate of Q2'25, further indicates a slower economy.

Inflation

We have thought for some time that the threat to inflation in the economy from the tariffs is considerably overblown and largely driven by media hand-wringing. We continue to believe that seller margins, not revenue, will be affected by President Trump’s tariffs, as we explained in this article from last November.

JP Morgan CEO Jamie Dimon, who had been critical of Trump’s tariffs, has come to align with that view. He reversed his view of tariffs as of yesterday, saying, “They may drive slightly higher inflation, but what you really want is more growth.” But Dimon's comments were made before this morning’s jobs report, although, as best as we can determine, after yesterday’s GDP report.

The greater risk to inflation, in our view, is the decline in the USD Index (DXY), which printed today at just 99.

Nevertheless, we continue to believe the ongoing collapse of China’s economy, which we discussed here, will cause China’s prices for manufactured goods to fall so that the cost of Chinese goods, after tariffs, will be within a couple of standard deviations of the 2024 average prices to American consumers. Walmart (WMT), which is known to aggressively push suppliers to lower wholesale prices, and we expect a number of other large U.S. importers will use the buyers' market to force their wholesale price from Chinese manufacturers down considerably.

The public also thinks inflation expectations seem to be overblown, with the 5-year inflation expectation basically at or around 2.32, where it was when Trump was sworn in, but up considerably from where it was in April, when tariff policy was first announced. It does seem to be declining after recent tariff agreements were announced with South Korea, the EU, and others.

Speaking of which, Europe seems to have changed its view with respect to the United States and President Trump, at least at the current impression. The flawless execution of Operation Midnight Hammer, the clandestine bombing of Iran’s nuclear sites. Europe now seems far more willing to accommodate and deal with the Trump governing agenda, as disruptive to the status quo as it may be. That said, France and, to a lesser extent, Germany seem to still oppose the Trump presidency.

Prognostications

The data point to a slowing economy. However, as we said in our 2025Q2 GDP report earlier this week, we think there may be substantially increased capex owing to the Big Beautiful Bill. However, that won’t be reflected, likely, until the later part of September and will not be reflected in 2025Q3 GDP. In view of today’s release, we are reducing our estimate of 2025 Q3 to just 1%, +/- 25 bps. We expect the Fed to lower rates by a quarter point in its September meeting.

Readers wishing a deeper dive on some of the other data points that affect our judgements are welcome to assess "Other Data Points" below.

Other Data Points

These are data points we use to assess the overall standing of the economy and are provided here for information only.

GDP printed at 3% in Q2'25, according to the advance estimate released July 30th. Core GDP, or final sales to domestic purchasers, which eliminates some of the “noise” in the GDP report, like inventories and net exports, printed at 1.2%, down from 1.9% in the 2025Q1. As we wrote in our GDP analysis earlier this week, there was a lot of “noise” in the GDP print owing to imports and inventories.

Core PCE Inflation, ex-food and fuel, reportedly the Fed’s preferred measure of inflation, was released July 31 and printed at a 2.8% annual rate for the month of June. PCE inflation printed at 2.6% Those figures are up from 2.7% and 2.3%, respectively, from May.

The Institute for Supply Management's Manufacturers' Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) printed at 48, down a full point from 49.0 in June. The overall manufacturing sector has been declining faster for the last five months.

The June services Index grew to 50.8 from May’s 49.9. It will be a few days until the services index for July is released.

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) for May was released on Tuesday, with 275,000 fewer job openings than in May and 261,000 fewer hires.

Privately-owned housing units authorized by building permits in June were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,397,000. This is 0.2% above the revised May rate of 1,394,000 but is 4.4% below the June 2024 rate of 1,461,000.

Personal income increased $71.4 billion (0.3% at a monthly rate) in June, according to estimates released today by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $61.0 billion (0.3%), and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $69.9 billion (0.3%).

The PCE price index for June increased 2.6% from the same month one year ago. Excluding food and energy, the PCE price index increased 2.8% from one year ago. From the preceding month, the PCE price index for June increased 0.3%. Excluding food and energy, the PCE price index also increased 0.3%.

Personal income increased $71.4 billion (0.3% at a monthly rate) in June, according to estimates released today by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $61.0 billion (0.3%), and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $69.9 billion (0.3%).

RealClearMarkets/TIPP Economic Optimism Index for August has not been printed as of this writing. In July, it edged down from 49.2 in June to 48.6—a 1.2% decline.

_________________________________________

Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe are underperforming. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies that we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change).

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: Deserving Dip
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (NASDAQ:PANW) just shocked the markets with a major acquisition at a huge premium. The cybersecurity leader even suggests more deals are in the works to complete their platform goals. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock due to the subpar growth in comparison to the premium valuation.

CyberArk Deal

On January 30, Palo Alto Networks agreed to acquire CyberArk (CYBR) in a deal valued at $25 billion. The company surprised the market with the size of the acquisition and the minimal revenue acquired in the process.

The cybersecurity giant is paying $45 in cash plus 2.2005 shares of stock for each CyberArk share. The mostly stock deal definitely acknowledges Palo Alto is using premium equity to pay for a stock trading at a premium valuation.

CyberArk is a leader in Identity Security with a focus on Privileged Access Management (PAM). The deal suggests an expectation for AI to vastly boost the need for security access to systems due to the use of machines for Agentic AI, where AI agents dramatically increase the amount of identities to manage and distinguish between the security requirements of humans and AI agents. CyberArk reported more than 80x the machine identities to manage compared to humans.

The market forecast is for the Identity Security TAM to jump from $29 billion in 2025 to $47 billion by 2028 due to AI. Palo Alto Networks apparently had a gap in identity management, and CyberArk solved this issue, allowing the combined company to now target a combined $257 billion TAM in a few years.

While all of this sounds great, CyberArk was only targeting 2025 revenues of $1.3 billion. Along with the merger announcement, the company reported Q2 2025 sales grew by 46% to reach $328 million via a nearly $12 million beat.

Palo Alto Networks paid an incredible 19x sales targets. For this reason, the deal won't be free cash flow accretive until FY 2028.

The cybersecurity giant had a market cap of $136 billion when the deal was announced, so the dilution is somewhat minimal. Palo Alto Networks has 707 million diluted shares outstanding, and the deal will issue about 103 million shares to CyberArk shareholders.

With Palo Alto Networks collapsing to $170, the deal has dipped to only $419. CyberArk shareholders were promised a deal premium of 26% over the pre-offer announcement price of $380, and the stock is only up about 10% now.

Platformization Push

Palo Alto Networks continues to make a huge platformization push that hasn't led to improving financial results. The company went from 25% growth in FY 2023 prior to announcing the multi-platform growth to only 16% in the last reported quarter, with a target of 14% in the just ended FQ4.

The CyberArk deal and lack of product coverage in AI Identity Security reinforce the concern over a platform focus versus best of breed products. Palo Alto Networks has leading products in endpoints and other areas of cybersecurity, but the company lacks the full solutions, and new areas of importance always pop up with AI, making identity security even more important.

The stock has slipped over $30 from the recent highs, but Palo Alto Networks is still a $115 billion market cap. The standalone business targets FY 2025 revenues of $9.2 billion, reaching $10.5 billion in FY 2026.

Palo Alto trades at 11x FY 2026 revenue targets. Once adding the CyberArk dilution and the $1.6 billion in 2026 revenues, the combined company has a market value of $138 billion with a sales target in the $12 billion range, leaving the stock trading at an expensive 14x sales forecast.

The deal requires a cash payment of $45 per CyberArk share, leading to a payout of $2.3 billion. Palo Alto Networks already has a cash balance of $8.5 billion with annual free cash flow generation of over $3 billion, again suggesting the stock premiums were limiting the interest in cashing out at these levels despite an abundance of cash.

The company is likely to continue making additional acquisitions to complete the platform process after buying Protect AI earlier this year and other firms in prior years. Apparently, Palo Alto Networks can't build the systems internally with the reality of cybersecurity always having a hard time creating one system or platform to cover all security needs constantly pushing customers to stronger point products.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Palo Alto Networks remains an expensive stock without the required growth rates. Even after the stock has fallen over $30 from announcing the deal to acquire CyberArk, the stock is still expensive.

Investors should use any rallied to sell the stock.
